findings.title: ManipulableOracle: Anyone Can Set Prices — Oracle Fully Compromised
findings.description: The ManipulableOracle.setPrice() function has no access control whatsoever. Any external account or contract can call setPrice() to set the price of any token to any arbitrary value. Since FlashLoanVault uses this oracle for all collateral calculations, borrowing limits, and liquidation checks, this means any attacker can trivially manipulate the price to borrow without real collateral, prevent legitimate liquidations, or force liquidations of healthy positions.
findings.message: Complete protocol compromise. Attacker can: (1) Set token price to max uint256, add minimal collateral, borrow entire vault liquidity. (2) Set token price to 1, force-liquidate all healthy borrowers and steal their collateral for free. (3) Drain all depositor funds.
findings.recommendation: Add onlyOwner modifier: `function setPrice(address token, uint256 price) external { require(msg.sender == owner, 'Not owner'); prices[token] = price; }`. Better yet, use a decentralized TWAP oracle such as Chainlink or Uniswap V3 TWAP, with staleness checks.
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker takes a flash loan of a large amount of the quote token from a lending protocol.
2. Attacker dumps the borrowed token into the DEX pool, crashing the spot price.
3. Victim contract reads the manipulated spot price via getReserves() or similar — treats it as market price.
4. Attacker exploits the mispriced collateral: borrows against inflated collateral or liquidates positions unfairly.
5. Attacker swaps back to restore the price, repays the flash loan, and pockets the arbitrage profit.
6. Entire attack is atomic — completes within a single transaction with no capital at risk.

findings.title: Flash Loan Callback Success Not Checked — Receiver Can Execute Malicious Logic Without Consequence
findings.description: In flashLoan(), the external call `(bool success, ) = receiver.call(data)` does not check the `success` return value. The callback can revert internally (caught by the low-level call) or do nothing, and execution continues. This means: (a) intended callback logic may silently fail, and (b) the receiver contract can execute arbitrary logic during the call (including state manipulation) and then choose to not repay — though the final balance check should catch non-repayment, the lack of success check means error handling in the receiver is invisible to the vault.
findings.message: The flash loan proceeds even if the receiver's callback fails silently. Combined with the reentrancy opportunity and oracle manipulation, this is a key enabler for the full attack chain. Legitimate integrators cannot rely on their callback succeeding.
findings.recommendation: Require success: `require(success, 'Flash loan callback failed');` — Add this immediately after the receiver.call line.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker's executeAttack() reverts internally for some path. 2. Vault ignores the revert, just checks final balance. 3. If attacker pre-funded the vault slightly, the balance check passes and the loan is considered 'repaid'. 4. Attacker gets free execution of partial logic.

findings.title: Flash Loan + Oracle Manipulation: Borrow Vault's Entire Liquidity With Manipulated Collateral
findings.description: The borrow() function checks collateral using calculateCollateralValue() which reads a spot price from the ManipulableOracle. This price can be set by anyone (see vulnerability above). Even if the oracle had access control, using a spot/instantaneous price is vulnerable to flash loan manipulation in the same block/transaction. The borrow() function also does NOT check the flashLoanLock, meaning a borrower can borrow during an active flash loan execution, using temporarily inflated collateral values established within the same transaction.
findings.message: Attacker can borrow the entire vault liquidity (totalDeposits - totalBorrowed) against worthless or minimal collateral by inflating the oracle price during the same transaction.
findings.recommendation: 1. Use a TWAP oracle (Chainlink, Uniswap V3 TWAP with minimum 15-min window). 2. Add flashLoanLock check to borrow(): `require(!flashLoanLock, 'Cannot borrow during flash loan')`. 3. Implement checks-effects-interactions pattern throughout.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker calls vault.flashLoan(X, attackerContract, executeAttackCalldata). 2. Inside the callback: a. Call oracle.setPrice(token, HUGE_VALUE). b. Call vault.addCollateral(1 wei). c. Call vault.borrow(totalDeposits - totalBorrowed) — passes because collateral value is astronomically large. d. Now attacker holds all vault liquidity. e. Repay flash loan from separate funds or revert (if using separate attack vector). 3. All depositor funds are drained.

findings.title: Flash Loan + Oracle Manipulation: Force-Liquidate Healthy Positions
findings.description: The liquidate() function computes collateral value using the same manipulable spot oracle. By setting the token price to 1 (nearly zero), any attacker can make all positions appear undercollateralized and liquidate them, stealing all collateral. The liquidation sends the full collateral amount to the liquidator and zeroes out the borrowed amount — effectively gifting the collateral to the attacker.
findings.message: All user collateral can be stolen. Attacker acquires all collateral tokens for free (they don't need to supply anything to liquidate — they just call liquidate() after manipulating the price).
findings.recommendation: 1. Use manipulation-resistant oracle (TWAP). 2. Add slippage/price bounds to liquidation. 3. Require liquidators to repay the borrowed amount before receiving collateral.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker calls oracle.setPrice(token, 1). 2. All borrowers now have collateralValue = (collateral * 1) / 1e18 ≈ 0. 3. Attacker calls liquidate(victim) for each borrower. 4. Full collateral transferred to attacker, borrowed[victim] = 0. 5. Attacker repeats for all victims. 6. Oracle price restored.

findings.title: Liquidation Does Not Require Liquidator to Repay Debt
findings.description: The liquidate() function sets borrowed[user] = 0 and transfers collateral[user] to the liquidator without requiring the liquidator to repay any of the user's debt. The totalBorrowed accounting variable is also never decremented. This means: (1) Liquidators get free collateral without providing any funds. (2) totalBorrowed permanently overstates outstanding debt, breaking all liquidity checks. (3) The vault's token balance decreases (collateral sent out) but totalBorrowed stays high, eventually preventing all borrows.
findings.message: Any liquidator can steal all collateral from undercollateralized users for free. Protocol accounting permanently breaks as totalBorrowed grows unbounded without corresponding repayment.
findings.recommendation: Liquidator must repay the debt: `require(token.transferFrom(msg.sender, address(this), borrowed[user]), 'Repay failed'); totalBorrowed -= borrowed[user]; borrowed[user] = 0; collateral[user] = 0; token.transfer(msg.sender, liquidationAmount);` Also add a liquidation bonus mechanism properly.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. User has collateral=1000 tokens, borrowed=500 tokens (undercollateralized due to price drop). 2. Attacker calls liquidate(user). 3. Attacker receives 1000 tokens, pays nothing. 4. borrowed[user]=0 but totalBorrowed still includes the 500 — never decremented. 5. Repeat for all users.

findings.message: Function assigns ownership but may not be the constructor — in Solidity <0.4.22, constructors were functions with the contract name. A typo means anyone can call it and become owner.
findings.description: Function assigns ownership but may not be the constructor — in Solidity <0.4.22, constructors were functions with the contract name. A typo means anyone can call it and become owner.
findings.recommendation: Function assigns ownership but may not be the constructor — in Solidity <0.4.22, constructors were functions with the contract name. A typo means anyone can call it and become owner.

findings.message: Reentrancy in FlashLoanVault.flashLoan(uint256,address,bytes) (../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#59-83):
findings.description:
Reentrancy in [FlashLoanVault.flashLoan(uint256,address,bytes)](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L59-L83):
	External call allowing reentrancy:
	- [require(bool,string)(token.transfer(receiver,amount),Transfer failed)](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L69)
	Balance read before the call:
	- [balanceBefore = token.balanceOf(address(this))](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L63)
	Possible stale balance used after the call in a condition:
	- [require(bool,string)(balanceAfter >= balanceBefore + fee,Flash loan not repaid)](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L79)
		- stale variable `balanceBefore`
findings.recommendation: Apply Checks-Effects-Interactions pattern: update state BEFORE external calls. Use OpenZeppelin's ReentrancyGuard (`nonReentrant` modifier).
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker deploys a malicious contract with a fallback/receive function that calls back into the victim.
2. Attacker calls the victim's withdraw() function with a valid balance.
3. Victim sends ETH to attacker — attacker's fallback immediately calls withdraw() again before state is updated.
4. Re-entry loop drains the contract balance in a single transaction (no per-call state update blocks it).
5. Attack ends when gas runs out or balance is exhausted; attacker keeps all drained ETH.

findings.message: Contract locks Ether without a withdraw function.
findings.description: It appears that the contract includes a payable function to accept Ether but lacks a corresponding function to withdraw it, which leads to the Ether being locked in the contract. To resolve this issue, please implement a public or external function that allows for the withdrawal of Ether from the contract.
findings.recommendation: Review and fix the issue according to best practices

findings.title: Boolean Reentrancy Lock Insufficient — Exploitable Via Same-Transaction State Manipulation
findings.description: The flashLoanLock is a boolean that is set to true during flash loan execution. However, borrow(), deposit(), withdraw(), addCollateral(), removeCollateral(), and repay() do NOT check flashLoanLock. This means during the flash loan callback, an attacker can call any of these functions freely, manipulating vault state (inflating collateral, borrowing, withdrawing deposits) before the flash loan repayment check. The lock only prevents nested flashLoan calls, not other state-changing operations.
findings.message: Attacker can perform arbitrary state manipulation during flash loan execution, enabling collateral inflation attacks, unauthorized borrowing, and deposit manipulation within a single transaction.
findings.recommendation: Use OpenZeppelin's ReentrancyGuard with nonReentrant modifier on ALL state-changing functions. Alternatively add `require(!flashLoanLock)` to borrow(), deposit(), withdraw(), addCollateral(), removeCollateral().
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker deploys a malicious contract with a fallback/receive function that calls back into the victim.
2. Attacker calls the victim's withdraw() function with a valid balance.
3. Victim sends ETH to attacker — attacker's fallback immediately calls withdraw() again before state is updated.
4. Re-entry loop drains the contract balance in a single transaction (no per-call state update blocks it).
5. Attack ends when gas runs out or balance is exhausted; attacker keeps all drained ETH.

findings.title: withdraw() Does Not Verify Available Liquidity Accounting for Outstanding Borrows
findings.description: The withdraw() function checks `deposits[msg.sender] >= amount` and `totalDeposits >= amount`, but does NOT check whether the actual token balance of the vault is sufficient. The vault's token balance can be less than totalDeposits because tokens have been lent out via borrow(). A depositor can attempt to withdraw funds that are currently lent out, which will fail at the token.transfer() call but only after state has been mutated (deposits and totalDeposits already decremented). While the revert will roll back state, this creates a DoS where users cannot withdraw during high borrow utilization even though their accounting shows available funds.
findings.message: Depositors cannot withdraw when borrow utilization is high. The check `totalDeposits - totalBorrowed >= amount` is missing, creating misleading state and potential accounting errors if the revert behavior is ever bypassed.
findings.recommendation: Add liquidity check: `require(totalDeposits - totalBorrowed >= amount, 'Insufficient available liquidity');` before state mutation.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. 1000 tokens deposited by users, totalDeposits=1000. 2. 900 tokens borrowed, totalBorrowed=900. Vault holds 100 tokens. 3. Depositor tries to withdraw 500. 4. deposits[user] >= 500 check passes. 5. totalDeposits (1000) >= 500 check passes. 6. deposits[user] -= 500 and totalDeposits -= 500 (state mutated). 7. token.transfer fails (only 100 tokens available). 8. Entire tx reverts — state restored. But the UX is broken and the checks are misleading.

findings.message: External send/transfer inside require — if recipient reverts, entire function is blocked (DoS). Use pull pattern instead.
findings.description: External send/transfer inside require — if recipient reverts, entire function is blocked (DoS). Use pull pattern instead.
findings.recommendation: External send/transfer inside require — if recipient reverts, entire function is blocked (DoS). Use pull pattern instead.

findings.message: Unsafe ERC20 Operations should not be used
findings.description: ERC20 functions may not behave as expected. For example: return values are not always meaningful. It is recommended to use OpenZeppelin's SafeERC20 library.
findings.recommendation: Review and fix the issue according to best practices

findings.message: Low-level call without explicit success check — use require(success) or SafeERC20.
findings.description: Low-level call without explicit success check — use require(success) or SafeERC20.
findings.recommendation: Low-level call without explicit success check — use require(success) or SafeERC20.
exploit_scenario: 1. Contract calls an external address using .call() or .send() but does not check the boolean return value.
2. The external call silently fails (e.g. recipient is a contract that reverts, or gas stipend is exceeded).
3. Because the return value is ignored, the contract continues execution and marks the operation as successful.
4. Tokens or ETH are never delivered, but the contract's internal state (balances, flags) is updated as if they were.
5. Attacker exploits the accounting discrepancy to double-spend or claim funds they should not have.

findings.message: Contract locking ether found:
findings.description:
Contract locking ether found:
	Contract [FlashLoanAttacker](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L219-L252) has payable functions:
	 - [FlashLoanAttacker.receive()](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L251)
	But does not have a function to withdraw the ether
findings.recommendation: Provide a withdrawal function or ensure contract is payable

findings.message: Unsafe ERC20 Operations should not be used
findings.description: ERC20 functions may not behave as expected. For example: return values are not always meaningful. It is recommended to use OpenZeppelin's SafeERC20 library.
findings.recommendation: Review and fix the issue according to best practices

findings.message: Unsafe ERC20 Operations should not be used
findings.description: ERC20 functions may not behave as expected. For example: return values are not always meaningful. It is recommended to use OpenZeppelin's SafeERC20 library.
findings.recommendation: Review and fix the issue according to best practices

findings.message: Approval pattern susceptible to front-running race condition. Consider increaseAllowance/decreaseAllowance.
findings.description: Approval pattern susceptible to front-running race condition. Consider increaseAllowance/decreaseAllowance.
findings.recommendation: Approval pattern susceptible to front-running race condition. Consider increaseAllowance/decreaseAllowance.
exploit_scenario: 1. Victim calls token.approve(spender, 100) to change an existing approval from 50 to 100.
2. Attacker monitors the mempool and sees the approve transaction before it is mined.
3. Attacker front-runs by calling transferFrom(victim, attacker, 50) while the old allowance of 50 is still active.
4. After the victim's approve is mined, attacker calls transferFrom again using the new allowance of 100.
5. Attacker steals 150 tokens even though the victim intended to grant only 100.
6. Fix: use increaseAllowance/decreaseAllowance which are atomic and race-free.

findings.message: transfer()/send() forwards only 2300 gas — will fail with contract recipients after EIP-1884. Use call{value:}() with reentrancy guard.
findings.description: transfer()/send() forwards only 2300 gas — will fail with contract recipients after EIP-1884. Use call{value:}() with reentrancy guard.
findings.recommendation: transfer()/send() forwards only 2300 gas — will fail with contract recipients after EIP-1884. Use call{value:}() with reentrancy guard.

findings.message: State-changing function without event emission — limits off-chain monitoring and indexing.
findings.description: State-changing function without event emission — limits off-chain monitoring and indexing.
findings.recommendation: State-changing function without event emission — limits off-chain monitoring and indexing.

findings.message: Unsafe ERC20 Operations should not be used
findings.description: ERC20 functions may not behave as expected. For example: return values are not always meaningful. It is recommended to use OpenZeppelin's SafeERC20 library.
findings.recommendation: Review and fix the issue according to best practices

findings.message: Approval pattern susceptible to front-running race condition. Consider increaseAllowance/decreaseAllowance.
findings.description: Approval pattern susceptible to front-running race condition. Consider increaseAllowance/decreaseAllowance.
findings.recommendation: Approval pattern susceptible to front-running race condition. Consider increaseAllowance/decreaseAllowance.
exploit_scenario: 1. Victim calls token.approve(spender, 100) to change an existing approval from 50 to 100.
2. Attacker monitors the mempool and sees the approve transaction before it is mined.
3. Attacker front-runs by calling transferFrom(victim, attacker, 50) while the old allowance of 50 is still active.
4. After the victim's approve is mined, attacker calls transferFrom again using the new allowance of 100.
5. Attacker steals 150 tokens even though the victim intended to grant only 100.
6. Fix: use increaseAllowance/decreaseAllowance which are atomic and race-free.

findings.message: transfer()/send() forwards only 2300 gas — will fail with contract recipients after EIP-1884. Use call{value:}() with reentrancy guard.
findings.description: transfer()/send() forwards only 2300 gas — will fail with contract recipients after EIP-1884. Use call{value:}() with reentrancy guard.
findings.recommendation: transfer()/send() forwards only 2300 gas — will fail with contract recipients after EIP-1884. Use call{value:}() with reentrancy guard.

findings.message: State-changing function without event emission — limits off-chain monitoring and indexing.
